description:
You are a student preparing for an environmental science presentation. You have a raw GPS track from a recent field trip to the Inca Trail, saved as a CSV file at `/home/ga/Documents/inca_trail_track.csv`.

Your task is to write a script to convert this CSV into a valid Keyhole Markup Language (KML) file so it can be viewed in a mapping application.

1. Open the Sugar Terminal (or Pippy activity).
2. Write a Python or Bash script and save it to `/home/ga/Documents/kml_generator.py` (or `kml_generator.sh`).
3. The script must read `/home/ga/Documents/inca_trail_track.csv`, skip the header row, and extract the coordinate data.
4. The script must generate a valid KML file saved exactly to `/home/ga/Documents/route.kml`.
5. The output KML must match this XML structure:

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<kml xmlns="http://www.opengis.net/kml/2.2">
  <Document>
    <name>Inca Trail Field Trip</name>
    <Placemark>
      <name>GPS Track</name>
      <LineString>
        <coordinates>
          longitude,latitude,elevation
          ...
        </coordinates>
      </LineString>
    </Placemark>
  </Document>
</kml>
```

The coordinates must be in `longitude,latitude,elevation` order (KML standard), with one coordinate tuple per line inside the `<coordinates>` element.
